text: Hey everyone. Uh it's a real pleasure to be here today. Over the past few years, I have had the chance to watch numerous fascinating talks right here at the AtScale podium. And just this morning listening to Boris uh talking about coding agents, such a treat. It's a real humbling experience to be here talking in front of you all today. What I plan to talk today is um about agents, of course, and the intent gap um and how to close that. Now, I'm sure um most, if not all, in the audience has had this experience. Um we built an agent on our laptop. We test it for its behavior. We launched it in the production, and then we saw that the agent did something that we never planned for the agent to do. Why does that happen? That's precisely what I plan to talk today about. And the series of steps and tools that can actually fix the problem. And I want to convince you by the end of the talk that this is really not a prompting problem. This is really a controls problem. All right, let's dive right into it. So, let's take a particular failure case. Let's say we are interested in building a library agent. Now, this agent needs to know who you are as a user, needs to understand your profiles, your preferences, needs to send you reminders whenever you are due to submit the book, and all the good things that you want your library agent to do, you have built it. Now, let's say there are some rules that you want your agent to adhere to. One such rule is that if the books have been recalled, they should not be renewed. So, what do you do as an agent builder? You go back to the system prompt of your agent and you write this natural language rule telling the agent what it should do under what circumstances. Now you test the behavior of your agent on your local setup. You you're convinced that your agent is doing the right thing and then you deploy the agent in the production. What you see is that often times your agent is renewing the books that have been recalled. Now why does that happen? Well, it happens because the rule that you intended your agent to stick to was written as a natural language prompt in the system prompt. Your agent never executed a tool to check the status of the book. And so this is the gap that we are talking about. Is the gap between what the agent builder intended for the agent to do versus what the agent actually executed in the production. Now the title of my talk says the intent gap singular. Well, I lied. There are actually two gaps. The first gap is the operator gap or the specification gap. Is the distance between what I intend the agent to do and what the agent does in the production. The second gap is the understanding gap. Is the user understanding gap. Is the distance between what the user says and what they actually mean. Now in all honesty, the understanding gap is a significantly harder gap to close. So what I plan to do today for the remainder of the talk I'm going to be closing on the specification gap. However, at the end I will come back and talk about some of the mitigation mitigation strategies that will mitigate or close the user understanding gap to a great extent if not fully. Now, how do you how do you guide the behavior of the agent at scale? It's an entire spectrum. On one end of the spectrum, you have prompts. And we saw in our library agent the rules were codified as a natural language instruction in the system prompt. Then you have standard operating procedures. You have steering hooks, something that I plan to talk about today. You have rigid workflows. And then on the other extreme end, you have external policy. So, these are different levers that shape the behavior of the agent. What I plan to do is take all these behavior traits and then collapse them onto two axes. And these axes matter more than anything else. The first dimension in this axis is where does the controls live? Does it live inside the agent loop? Or does it live outside? And the second one, and this is something that I want to I want you to hold on to for the remainder of the talk is upon detecting that your agent is about to misbehave, what is it that you would like to do? Would you like to course correct, nudge the model so that it has another opportunity to do the right thing? Or do you block the operation? Now, the two corrective behaviors, they sit at the opposite end of the spectrum. Steering is within loop nudging behavior. You are basically giving the model a chance to retry and redo upon understanding that the agent is misbehaving. On the diametrically opposite end of the behavior spectrum, you have policy engine. In policy engine, there are there is no room for negotiation. There is no room for retry. It's a hard block. Whether you agree that the agent should execute or you block that action. Now, you may be wondering why I do not have SOPs or standard operating procedures and prompts on those access. The reason for that is that SOPs and prompts, they shape the behavior of the agent during build time. While what I'm interested in is shaping the behavior of the agent during run time. And that is where steering and external policy come into the picture. For the remainder of the talk, I will dive deep into one way of implementing some of these steering hooks and one way of implementing the external policy. Now, let's look at um a particular implementation of how you can steer the model. Now, the steering hook in our implementation, it fires at two places. The first one is right before the tool call and the second is right after the model has given a response. So, now let's go back to our bug um when we were building our library agent, the bug that we saw uh we were noticing in our production was that the agent was uh renewing the books that were um recalled. This is not the behavior we want our agent to uh to adhere to. So, now when we observe this behavior in in the non-steering world, what you would do is you would go back to the system prompt and you would add couple notes saying, "Hey agent, you should not or you should." All in capitals, hoping that your agent is going to listen to you um especially when the instructions are written in all caps. Versus in the steering mechanism, what you have is that the handler has access to the entire ledger. It has access to all the tools that have been executed till that point and all the input and the corresponding output of all the tools. What that means is that right before calling renew book tool, the handler is going to look at whether get status was ever called. If not, then in the counterfactual world, in the non-steering world, you would have to go and edit the prompt, but in the steering world, you are going to guide the model. You are going to nudge the model. You are going to suggest to the model that, "Hey, it needs to check the status of the book before executing this tool." Now, if there is one verb that comes to my mind to define steering, that verb is guide. And that's the entire philosophy. You are giving model another chance to recover. You are giving the model gentle nudges so that it has another chance to do the right thing. Now, there is no free lunch and this is true even here. Anytime you are guiding the model, you have tokens and inference fee on the production hot path. Similarly, for things that can't be codified, such as say tone or format, uh for that, you really don't have a deterministic way of writing a code. You have to use LLM as a judge. And unfortunately, LLM as a judge does not give you 100% guarantee. Versus that, for things that can be codified, you can actually unit test them. Uh what I showed in the previous slide is a deterministic piece of Python code. You can unit test that, which means that all the rules that you are testing in your local setup is guaranteed to work in production. There are no surprises. Now, let's talk about policy engine. Uh the the other extreme where you have to take um bit more stricter stance on what you want to do when you deem your agent is misbehaving. Now, in our implementation, policy engine sits at the gateway. So, anytime the agent has to make a call to the tool, it has to go through the gateway. And so, your gateway is either going to allow or it's going to deny. There is going to be no feedback. There is going to be um no opportunity for retry, no negotiations. Now, let's take a pause here. You may wonder uh why would anybody want to have a hard blocker outside the agentic reasoning loop when we have a very elegant in-loop reasoning logic that we just saw in the steering slides? Well, the reason for that is that your steering uh runs through model's reasoning loop. And anything that can run through model's reasoning loop can be reasoned with. So, if your enforcement logic sits in a component that can be argued with, your adversarial user gets to argue with it. And that's precisely what is That's precisely what you don't want for use cases that involve security and authorization. Now, you don't um the way the way you have to implement these policies um how do you go How do you achieve that? Um there are variety of ways you can um achieve writing policies that determine whether an agent should execute a certain task or not. The way we have implemented this is via using um an authorization language called Cedar. Doesn't have to be Cedar. Uh it's just one way of implementing it. Now, the operator, remember, the persona for whom we are solving this problem or we are building these tools, is the agent builder persona. So, they have certain intent in their mind. They want the agent to adhere to certain specifications. So, let's say for a particular agent, the intent that they we want the agent to adhere to is that the agent should allow appointment slots between 9:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. How do you go from this intent to a policy that is deterministic and verifiable? Now, the way we have done this is translate the queries, the natural language query, into a language that you can analyze. Now, you may be wondering, well, isn't natural language lossy and noisy? Wasn't that the problem to begin with? The whole problem of user intent is that natural language is ambiguous and therefore it's very hard to close the intent gap, especially the user understanding gap. So, then how is it that we are solving the problem of closing the gap by going back to natural language? You should remember that again, I have to remind that this is being built for the agent builder persona. Meaning, this is a one-time exercise, if you will. The beauty of giving the natural language lever to the user is that it's less overwhelming to them. They can express the intent in the natural language, but the verifiable language is something that they can is deterministic and then they can go through and check for the correctness. It's a It's a one-time exercise. So, particularly in the case of Setter, this language has principles, it has actions, it has resources, and it has constraints such as when. So, when you take a natural language intent and you translate that into um into Setter language, you can actually go through the constraints and make sure that uh the intent that you had in mind is getting translated correctly into into the policy. Now, between guiding and blocking is the same constraint, but there are two different verbs. And picking the wrong verb is a failure mode in itself. So, you block a reversible formatting slip of an agent, and you have wasted the entire agent run. Versus, if you if you guide a money movement or um an irreversible delete operation, then you are committing to uh an irreversible operation. So, which verb to pick is really a function of how reversible my action is, uh how adversarial my task is, uh my environment is, and how big of the blast radius I'm talking about. Now, the interesting thing is that you don't have to pick one over the other. You can actually route these constraints in a layered mechanism. The typical agentic workflows that we are talking about are complex, and they are they are huge, um and the and the complexity level is is high, meaning we have an opportunity to layer these constraints depending upon the scenario. So, for example, if you have a hard security or authorization boundary, no questions asked, we go with the policy engine. Versus, if you have deterministic parameter check required by the tools, uh depending upon the blast radius, one may choose to have steering, meaning giving nudge to the to the agent so that it course corrects, or if the blast radius is big, then we can block that operation. Similarly, tool ordering. Now, in steering, because the handler has access to the entire ledger, giving the nudge giving access of that ledger to the model allows the model to course correct. If there is a certain sequence of tools that you want your agent to adhere to, giving access giving agents the access to the ledger allows the agent to course correct and understand what is the right tool ordering. If it has if it has made a mistake in picking the right tools for the right task. Similarly, for tone and format and judgment, steering is the way to go. However, for things such as tone and judgment, there is no deterministic way of ensuring that the agent will do the right thing. You have to resort to LLM as a judge, which unfortunately does not give you 100% guarantee. Now, at the beginning of the talk, I said there are two gaps. The first one is the specification gap or the operator gap. And I spent majority of the time talking about what that gap is and what are some of the mechanisms that can close the gap. And I said I'll come back and talk about the user intent gap. So, let's talk about that. So, as a reminder, user intent gap is the understanding gap. Is the distance between what the user said and what they actually meant. It's a much harder gap to close because again, you know, it's it's trying [snorts] to understand what the user is trying to say to the agent. But there are some best practices that at least in our work have proven very useful. I'm just going to walk you through some of those best practices. The first one is reading the reading the plan back to the user. So, if the user is interacting with the agent, it's always a good idea to give the plan back to the user for confirmation. Now, whenever you anticipate that your agent is about to make an irreversible or a costly operation, it's always a good idea to get confirmation from the user. Whenever there is ambiguity, whenever the agent is at a fork, and then there are multiple possibilities, get get the clarification from the user. And the best of all, just know your user. Capture the preferences both explicit and implicit. So, if your agent becomes a hyper-personalized agent, it doesn't have to re-elicit the intent. It already knows who the user is and what are the repeated use cases the user has with the with the agent. To close, I want to uh leave you with couple uh thoughts. The first one is that reliability and the predictability of agent is not a longer prompt problem. You You don't want to do whack-a-mole on a uh prompting treadmill. It's a control problem, and I hope you are convinced that longer prompt is not going to solve this problem. You need to have very structured layered control strategy to build these reliable agents. Second, choosing which verb to use at which layer is really a function of how reversible your task is, um and um how adversarial your environment is, uh and how big of the blast radius you really are talking about. And the third one, for the gaps that you really can't close with you know, user understanding being one of them, you need to be honest about how to manage them. At the end, I want to say that learning how to steer the model, learning when to block the model, we actually have a huge opportunity in front of us to enable the agents to take the step from being impressive to trustworthy. Now, imagine if you have scalable agents that are trustworthy, the type of productivity that they are going to unleash is going to be magical. And I'm really looking forward to that future where every developer, every team, every organization becomes highly productive virtue of these trustworthy scalable agents. Thank you so much for your attention.
